title: Origami rabbit face
subtitle: Folding a flat rabbit face from a single square
excerpt: The origami rabbit face is one of the most accessible recognisable models in the traditional repertoire. Produced in four to six folds from a single square, it gives a flat face silhouette with two pointed ears and a simple muzzle. The model is appropriate for complete beginners and is a good introduction to the valley and mountain fold system.
sourceNotes: Traditional origami model; the rabbit face appears in numerous early twentieth-century Japanese craft publications. The version described here follows the common four-fold construction documented in public-domain origami guides.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold toward the viewer, making a concave crease in the paper. The basic fold in most origami models. Represented in diagrams by a dashed line.
A crease running from one corner of a square to the opposite corner. Used as the starting fold for many origami models, establishing the paper's centre and axis of symmetry.
In this model, the fold that shapes the two ears of the rabbit face: a corner of the square is folded forward and upward at an angle to create a pointed ear shape.

content.text: as its main fold and adds two
for the muzzle. No special techniques are required beyond making clean, precise creases.

items.name: Kami origami paper, 15 cm
items.notes: One square per rabbit. White or pale colours suit the traditional rabbit face; colour side facing down to begin so the white or light side shows on the finished face.

content.text: Place the square colour side down, oriented as a diamond with one corner at the top. Fold the bottom corner up to meet the top corner, making a triangle. The colour side is now on the outside. Sharpen the fold along the full bottom edge.

With the triangle flat on the table, the long folded edge at the bottom and the point at the top: fold the left corner up and slightly to the left at a shallow angle, so it extends above the top edge of the triangle. This is the left ear. Repeat symmetrically on the right. The angle of the fold determines the ear shape: a steep angle makes tall narrow ears; a shallower angle makes wide, relaxed ears. Sharpen both ear folds.

Fold the bottom point of the triangle upward by approximately 15 to 20 mm to form the muzzle. Sharpen the fold. Turn the model over: the face is on the front. Add eyes and a nose with a pen if desired.

For a rabbit with floppy ears, fold the ear tips forward and down after step 2 before turning the model over. For a series of rabbits in different colours displayed together, vary the ear angle slightly in each one so each has a distinct character. The model folds quickly at 15 cm; at 7.5 cm it makes a small decorative motif suitable for cards or gift tags.
